title: The Gnostic Gospels Collection
author: Various (Nag Hammadi Texts)
description: Five suppressed gospels — Judas, Mary, Philip, Truth, and four companion texts — recovered from the Egyptian desert in 1945. A Christianity that was not lost by accident.

title: The Consolation of Philosophy
author: Boethius
description: Written in prison the night before his execution, Boethius argues with Lady Philosophy about fortune, fate, and the good. It has steadied the minds of condemned men for fifteen centuries.

title: Hypatia of Alexandria
author: Maria Dzielska
description: A rigorous dismantling of the myth to find the real woman: philosopher, mathematician, head of the Platonist school in Alexandria, and murdered by a Christian mob in 415 CE. History rarely recovers its victims this well.

title: Liber Novus
author: C.G. Jung
description: Jung's private record of his deliberate descent into the unconscious, kept hidden for nearly a century. Every major idea he published afterward began somewhere in these illuminated pages.

title: The Cloud of Unknowing and Other Works
author: Anonymous (14th Century)
description: An anonymous English mystic teaches that God cannot be reached by thought or feeling — only by a naked intent of love aimed into a cloud of unknowing. One of the strangest and truest books in the language.

title: The Master and His Emissary
author: Iain McGilchrist
description: The two hemispheres of the brain do not split tasks — they attend to reality in opposite ways, and one of them has been slowly winning. McGilchrist argues this has shaped Western civilization, and not for the better.

title: Stalking the Wild Pendulum
author: Itzhak Bentov
description: A biomedical engineer applies the tools of physics to the mechanics of consciousness — altered states, the structure of the universe, and the possibility that awareness runs deeper than we assume. Genuinely strange and genuinely rigorous.

title: Thus Spoke Zarathustra
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
description: Nietzsche's prophet descends from a mountain to announce the death of God and the uncertain task of what comes next. What you find in it depends almost entirely on what you bring.

title: Man's Search for Meaning
author: Viktor Frankl
description: A psychiatrist's account of survival in the Nazi concentration camps and the understanding it forced on him: that meaning, not pleasure or power, is the primary human need. The shortest great book I have ever read.

title: Physics
author: Isaac McFee
description: An excellent gateway into fundamental physics covering quantum mechanics, relativity, and their famous unresolved conflict — perfect for self-learners of any age who are curious about how the universe actually works.

title: Morphic Resonance
author: Rupert Sheldrake
description: Sheldrake proposes that nature has memory — that the habits of organisms, from crystal formation to animal behavior, are shaped by invisible fields inherited from all past members of their kind. One of the most genuinely heterodox ideas in modern biology, argued with scientific rigor.

title: Mathematics
author: Isaac McFee
description: The companion volume to McFee's Physics, tracing mathematics from its foundations in logic and number through calculus, topology, and the unsolved problems that keep the field alive. Written for the curious reader who suspects that mathematics is more strange and beautiful than school ever suggested.

title: Joan of Arc: A History
author: Helen Castor
description: A rigorously sourced account of the peasant girl who claimed divine voices, led armies, and was burned as a heretic at nineteen. Castor strips away five centuries of myth-making to recover the actual political and religious world Joan moved through — and why that world could not survive her.

title: Meditations
author: Marcus Aurelius
description: A Roman emperor's private journal, written with no intention of publication, recording his daily attempts to live according to Stoic principles — to remain undisturbed, to act justly, to remember what matters and what does not. The most practiced and least performed of all philosophical texts.

title: Pistis Sophia
author: Anonymous (Gnostic Text)
description: The most complete surviving Gnostic scripture, recording the post-resurrection teachings of Jesus on the architecture of the heavens and the soul's long return to the light. Read alongside the Nag Hammadi texts.
